entries.title: Goodbye, things
entries.author: Fumio Sasaki

classification.topic: Minimalism & Intentional Living
classification.subject: productivity-self-improvement

atomicUnits.title: Possessions silently demand your attention
atomicUnits.body: Every object you own emits a low-level signal that consumes mental energy — reminding you to maintain it, store it, or feel guilty about not using it. Reducing the number of things you own frees up the cognitive bandwidth those silent messages were consuming.

atomicUnits.title: Things as outsourced self-worth
atomicUnits.body: People accumulate possessions to broadcast their value to others, treating objects as proxies for the qualities they fear they lack. The accumulation reveals an internal deficit it can never actually fill, because external signals don't change how you regard yourself.

atomicUnits.title: Discard by questioning ownership reasons
atomicUnits.body: For each item, ask why you own it and whether you'd acquire it again today at full price; keep only what survives. Replace storage of 'might need someday' items with the willingness to re-acquire them on the rare occasion they're actually needed.

atomicUnits.title: Habituation erases the joy of new things
atomicUnits.body: Any acquisition delivers a spike of pleasure that fades as the object becomes background, so chasing happiness through buying creates an endless treadmill. Owning less interrupts this cycle by removing the constant supply of things to adapt to.

atomicUnits.title: Saved time and space as compound returns
atomicUnits.body: View each removed possession not as a one-time loss but as a recurring dividend — the cleaning, organizing, and deciding it no longer requires pays out repeatedly over time. Minimalism is less about the moment of discarding and more about the accumulated freedom afterward.

atomicUnits.title: Fewer choices increase available focus
atomicUnits.body: Owning a small, fixed set of items eliminates the daily micro-decisions about what to wear, use, or keep, conserving willpower for what matters. The constraint becomes liberating because the energy spent selecting among options gets redirected toward meaningful action.

entries.reviewReason: no Wikipedia entry found — decomposition based on description only

entries.title: Ganbatte!
entries.author: Albert Liebermann

classification.topic: Japanese Philosophy of Perseverance & Forward Motion
classification.subject: eastern-philosophy-zen

atomicUnits.title: Ganbatte as Effort Without Outcome Attachment
atomicUnits.body: Ganbatte means giving your best effort in the present moment without fixating on the result, shifting the locus of pride from winning to wholehearted participation. The reward is located in the quality of engagement itself, which makes the effort sustainable regardless of external success or failure.

atomicUnits.title: Kaizen as Continuous Tiny Improvement
atomicUnits.body: Progress is achieved through small, incremental adjustments repeated consistently rather than dramatic overhauls, because tiny changes meet less internal resistance and compound over time. The mechanism works by lowering the activation threshold so action begins before motivation is fully present.

atomicUnits.title: Perseverance Is Cultural, Not Just Personal
atomicUnits.body: The willingness to keep going is shaped by language and social encouragement — saying 'ganbatte' to one another embeds persistence as a shared expectation rather than a private struggle. This reframes endurance as a collective practice that members reinforce in each other instead of an isolated act of willpower.

atomicUnits.title: Forward Motion Over Stationary Reflection
atomicUnits.body: When facing difficulty, taking the next concrete step is prioritized over waiting for clarity or certainty, because momentum generates the information and confidence that planning alone cannot. Action itself becomes the diagnostic tool that reveals the path.

atomicUnits.title: Obstacles as Material for Mastery
atomicUnits.body: Rather than viewing hardship as an interruption to a smooth path, it is reframed as the necessary raw material through which skill and character are forged. This lens converts setbacks from evidence of failure into expected components of any worthwhile process.

atomicUnits.title: Acceptance as Foundation for Striving
atomicUnits.body: Genuine perseverance begins by accepting present reality and one's limitations honestly rather than denying them, which paradoxically frees energy that denial would otherwise consume. Striving is most effective when it builds on an unflinching acknowledgment of where one actually stands.

entries.reviewReason: no Wikipedia entry found — decomposition based on description only

entries.title: The Japanese mind
entries.author: Roger J. Davies

classification.topic: Japanese Culture & Social Psychology
classification.subject: genius-cultural-history

atomicUnits.title: Honne and Tatemae as Dual Truth
atomicUnits.body: Japanese social interaction distinguishes honne (one's true inner feelings) from tatemae (the publicly stated position required by social context). This is not seen as hypocrisy but as a necessary mechanism for preserving group harmony, where suppressing personal opinion in favor of the situationally appropriate response is a social skill rather than a moral failing.

atomicUnits.title: Amae as Indulgent Dependence
atomicUnits.body: Amae is the desire to be passively loved and indulged, modeled on the mother-child bond and extended into adult relationships and hierarchies. It binds individuals through mutual reliance — a subordinate presumes upon a superior's benevolence, and the superior accepts the dependence — making interdependence rather than autonomy the basis of secure relationships.

atomicUnits.title: Uchi-Soto Insider-Outsider Boundary
atomicUnits.body: Behavior is calibrated by whether someone belongs to one's uchi (inside group) or soto (outside group), and these boundaries shift with context — family, company, or nation. Politeness language, openness, and obligation all change depending on which side of the line the other person falls, making identity relational rather than fixed.

atomicUnits.title: Harmony Maintained Through Conflict Avoidance
atomicUnits.body: Wa (group harmony) is preserved by avoiding direct confrontation, suppressing dissent, and reaching decisions through gradual consensus-building rather than open debate. The cost of explicit disagreement is judged higher than the cost of inefficiency or unexpressed objection, so smoothness of relations takes priority over individual assertion.

atomicUnits.title: Reading Silence as Communication
atomicUnits.body: Meaning is carried heavily by what is left unsaid, by pauses, and by the listener's responsibility to intuit the speaker's intent (haragei, or 'belly talk'). Explicit verbal articulation can signal immaturity or distrust, so the skilled communicator infers context rather than demanding clear statements.

atomicUnits.title: Obligation Structured by Giri and Ninjo
atomicUnits.body: Social behavior is governed by the tension between giri (duty and obligation owed to others within a network of reciprocity) and ninjo (natural human feeling or compassion). One incurs lasting debts through favors received and must repay them appropriately, while personal emotion may pull against formal duty, creating recurring moral dilemmas.

entries.reviewReason: no Wikipedia entry found — decomposition based on description only

entries.title: Little Book of Ikigai
entries.author: Ken Mogi

classification.topic: Ikigai: Japanese Framework for Meaning & Motivation
classification.subject: eastern-philosophy-zen

atomicUnits.title: Ikigai Spans Small Joys and Life Purpose
atomicUnits.body: Ikigai is not limited to grand life missions; it equally describes the small daily pleasures that make getting up worthwhile, like a morning coffee or sensory ritual. This dual scope means meaning can be cultivated through micro-satisfactions, not only through achieving a singular purpose.

atomicUnits.title: The Five Pillars of Ikigai
atomicUnits.body: Ikigai is sustained by five foundations: starting small, releasing yourself (accepting who you are), harmony and sustainability, the joy of little things, and being in the here and now. Each pillar functions independently, so a person can enter ikigai through any one without needing all simultaneously.

atomicUnits.title: Ikigai Does Not Require Income or Recognition
atomicUnits.body: Unlike Western framings that link purpose to career or financial reward, ikigai can be fully realized in pursuits that generate no money and earn no public acclaim. The value comes from the intrinsic engagement itself, decoupling meaning from external validation or economic productivity.

atomicUnits.title: Sushi Apprentice's Decade of Rice
atomicUnits.body: A trainee sushi chef may spend years doing seemingly menial tasks like preparing rice before being allowed to handle fish, finding ikigai in the incremental mastery of each stage. The pleasure lies in the disciplined process and gradual perfection, not in rushing toward the prestigious endpoint.

atomicUnits.title: Flow as the Engine of Absorption
atomicUnits.body: When attention is fully absorbed in an activity matched to one's skill, the sense of self and time dissolves, producing the psychological state of flow. Viewing pursuits through this lens reveals that ikigai often arises precisely in moments where self-consciousness disappears into the task.

atomicUnits.title: Releasing Yourself to Accept Reality
atomicUnits.body: A core move in ikigai is letting go of the ego's resistance and accepting one's actual circumstances and nature rather than fighting them. This self-acceptance frees energy that would otherwise be spent on self-judgment, allowing genuine engagement with what is in front of you.

entries.reviewReason: no Wikipedia entry found — decomposition based on description only

entries.title: The Wabi Sabi Way
entries.author: Mike Sturm

classification.topic: Wabi-Sabi: Applying Japanese Aesthetics of Imperfection to Everyday Life
classification.subject: eastern-philosophy-zen
classification.author_tradition: Writing on Japanese aesthetics applied to modern living

atomicUnits.title: Wabi-Sabi as Acceptance of Impermanence
atomicUnits.body: Wabi-sabi is the aesthetic and disposition that finds beauty in things that are imperfect, incomplete, and impermanent. Rather than chasing polish and permanence, it trains attention on the natural cycle of decay, aging, and transience as the actual condition of all things.

atomicUnits.title: Perfectionism Defers Living Indefinitely
atomicUnits.body: Pursuing a flawless ideal postpones satisfaction to a finish line that never arrives, because every achievement reveals a new imperfection to fix. Accepting imperfection as the permanent baseline is what allows a person to actually inhabit their life now instead of waiting for it to begin.

atomicUnits.title: Flaws as Markers of Authenticity
atomicUnits.body: Viewing cracks, wear, and irregularity not as defects but as evidence of history and use changes what you value in an object or experience. The signs of age and damage become the very things that make something singular and real rather than the things that diminish it.

atomicUnits.title: Subtraction Toward the Essential
atomicUnits.body: Improvement comes from removing what is excessive, ornamental, or distracting rather than adding more. By stripping a space, schedule, or possession down to only what genuinely matters, you reveal a quieter clarity that addition tends to bury.

atomicUnits.title: Beauty in the Modest and Overlooked
atomicUnits.body: Wabi-sabi locates value in the humble, the rough, and the unremarkable rather than the grand or expensive. Attending to small, ordinary, easily-ignored details retrains perception to find richness in what most people walk past without noticing.

atomicUnits.title: Embracing Limits Reduces Suffering
atomicUnits.body: Resisting the unchangeable realities of aging, loss, and constraint generates more distress than the realities themselves. Aligning expectations with the inherently limited and transient nature of life converts a source of anxiety into a source of peace.

entries.reviewReason: no Wikipedia entry found — decomposition based on description only

entries.title: MCDP 2 Intelligence
entries.author: Department of Defense

classification.topic: Military Intelligence Doctrine & Decision Support
classification.subject: military-intelligence-doctrine

atomicUnits.title: Intelligence Cycle as Continuous Loop
atomicUnits.body: Intelligence operates as a cyclical process: planning and direction, collection, processing, production, and dissemination, with each output feeding back into new requirements. The cycle is not strictly sequential — phases overlap and run simultaneously, and feedback at any stage can redirect collection before the loop completes.

atomicUnits.title: Estimate Versus Fact Distinction
atomicUnits.body: Intelligence is fundamentally an estimate about an adaptive adversary, not a statement of verified fact, and must explicitly carry its degree of uncertainty. Presenting probabilistic judgment as certainty corrupts decisions, so analysts must communicate confidence levels and the assumptions underlying each conclusion.

atomicUnits.title: Intelligence Must Be Commander-Driven
atomicUnits.body: Intelligence efforts are wasted unless tied to the specific decisions a commander must make, so the commander must personally direct priorities rather than delegate them entirely. Intelligence that answers interesting questions instead of decision-relevant ones consumes scarce collection assets while leaving the actual choice unsupported.

entries.reviewReason: no Wikipedia entry found — decomposition based on description only

entries.title: MCDP 1 Warfighting
entries.author: Department of Defense

classification.topic: U.S. Marine Corps Doctrine on the Theory & Conduct of War
classification.subject: military-strategy-doctrine

atomicUnits.title: Friction as the Defining Feature of War
atomicUnits.body: War is dominated by friction — the countless small factors (fatigue, fear, weather, bad information, mechanical failure) that make even simple actions difficult and degrade plans in execution. Because friction cannot be eliminated, plans should be designed to be robust and adaptable under degradation rather than dependent on everything going right.

atomicUnits.title: Maneuver Warfare Targets Cohesion, Not Attrition
atomicUnits.body: Rather than destroying the enemy piece by piece through superior firepower, maneuver warfare seeks to shatter the enemy's cohesion and will by attacking critical vulnerabilities faster than they can respond. The aim is to create a cascading sequence of dilemmas that paralyzes decision-making, so the system collapses before its parts are physically defeated.

atomicUnits.title: Commander's Intent Enables Decentralized Action
atomicUnits.body: Subordinates are given the purpose and desired end state of an operation rather than detailed instructions, so they can act independently when the situation changes faster than orders can travel. This allows initiative at the lowest levels to remain aligned with overall goals even when communication breaks down or the original plan no longer fits reality.

entries.reviewReason: no Wikipedia entry found — decomposition based on description only

entries.title: MCDP 3 Expeditionary Operations
entries.author: Department of Defense

classification.topic: Marine Corps Doctrine on Expeditionary Operations
classification.subject: military-doctrine-strategy

atomicUnits.title: Expeditionary Mindset Over Material Readiness
atomicUnits.body: Being expeditionary is primarily a state of mind, not a function of equipment — it means a willingness to deploy rapidly into austere environments with whatever is at hand and to operate with self-sufficiency rather than waiting for ideal conditions. Forces that internalize this mentality stay light, mentally flexible, and ready to improvise, while forces that rely on accumulated infrastructure lose the capacity to move quickly.

atomicUnits.title: Tailoring Forces to the Mission
atomicUnits.body: Rather than deploying fixed standing units, capabilities are assembled into task-organized packages scaled precisely to the specific mission, then disassembled and recombined as the situation changes. This modular approach trades the efficiency of permanent structures for the adaptability of building exactly the force a problem requires.

atomicUnits.title: Crisis Response Depends on Forward Presence
atomicUnits.body: The decisive factor in responding to a crisis is not total combat power but the time it takes to arrive, so positioning capable forces forward — already near likely flashpoints — converts hours into the difference between shaping an event and reacting to its aftermath. Speed of arrival with a credible force often matters more than the eventual size of the response.

entries.reviewReason: no Wikipedia entry found — decomposition based on description only

entries.title: MCDP 5 Planning
entries.author: Department of Defense

classification.topic: Marine Corps Doctrine on Planning Theory & Practice
classification.subject: military-planning-doctrine

atomicUnits.title: Planning Is Continuous, Not a Product
atomicUnits.body: Planning is an ongoing process of learning and adapting, not the creation of a fixed document that ends when the plan is published. A plan is a snapshot of intentions at one moment, and its value lies in the shared understanding generated during planning rather than in the artifact itself.

atomicUnits.title: Tempo Through Decentralized Execution
atomicUnits.body: Plans should specify intent and desired end state while leaving subordinates maximum freedom to determine how to achieve it. This decentralization increases the speed of decision-making across the organization, allowing it to act faster than an opponent who waits for centralized direction.

atomicUnits.title: Plans Reduce But Cannot Eliminate Uncertainty
atomicUnits.body: Planning manages uncertainty by identifying what can be reasonably anticipated and building in branches and sequels for contingencies, rather than attempting to predict the future precisely. Because complex situations are inherently unpredictable, a good plan preserves flexibility instead of locking in a single rigid course of action.

entries.reviewReason: no Wikipedia entry found — decomposition based on description only

entries.title: MCDP 4 Logistics
entries.author: Department of Department of Defense

classification.topic: Marine Corps Doctrine on Logistics & Sustainment
classification.subject: military-logistics

atomicUnits.title: Logistics As Operational Reach Limiter
atomicUnits.body: The scope and tempo of any operation is bounded by the logistics that can sustain it, meaning logistics does not merely support strategy but actively constrains what strategy is possible. Commanders who plan operations beyond their sustainment capacity create culminating points where momentum collapses regardless of tactical success.

atomicUnits.title: Pull Versus Push Distribution Logic
atomicUnits.body: Supply systems can either push standardized packages forward on a schedule or respond to actual demand pulled by the units consuming resources. Demand-based pull reduces waste and matches real needs but requires accurate, fast information flow; anticipatory push guarantees availability but accumulates excess and stagnant stockpiles when forecasts miss.

atomicUnits.title: Responsiveness Beats Mass In Sustainment
atomicUnits.body: A smaller, faster, more flexible logistics flow outperforms a large static stockpile because speed of resupply lets units carry less and move freely. Accumulating mountains of supplies creates a vulnerable, immobile target and ties forces to fixed positions, so the goal is velocity of delivery rather than volume of inventory.

entries.reviewReason: no Wikipedia entry found — decomposition based on description only

entries.title: MCDP 6 Command and Control
entries.author: Department of Department of Defense

classification.topic: Military Doctrine of Command and Control in Warfare
classification.subject: command-control-decision-making

atomicUnits.title: Decision Speed Beats Decision Quality
atomicUnits.body: In a conflict between two adapting systems, the side that cycles through observation, orientation, decision, and action faster forces its opponent to react to outdated situations, generating cumulative confusion. Speed of decision relative to the enemy matters more than the optimality of any single decision, because a fast stream of adequate decisions degrades the opponent's ability to keep up.

atomicUnits.title: Mission Command Through Commander's Intent
atomicUnits.body: Subordinates are given the purpose and desired end state of an operation rather than detailed instructions on execution, allowing them to act independently when conditions change or communication breaks down. This decentralizes initiative to the lowest levels while preserving unity of effort, because everyone understands the 'why' that lets them improvise toward the same goal.

atomicUnits.title: Command As Managing Uncertainty Not Eliminating It
atomicUnits.body: War is fundamentally characterized by friction, chance, and incomplete information, so command systems should be designed to function effectively amid uncertainty rather than to acquire perfect knowledge before acting. Attempts to gather complete information before deciding create paralysis, so the goal is to make uncertainty tolerable and act despite it.

entries.reviewReason: no Wikipedia entry found — decomposition based on description only

entries.title: One man's view of the world
entries.author: Lee Kuan Yew

classification.topic: Geopolitical Analysis of Global Powers & Asia's Future
classification.subject: geopolitics-world-order

atomicUnits.title: China Will Not Adopt Liberal Democracy
atomicUnits.body: China's leaders see one-party rule as essential to managing a continent-sized population, and they fear the chaos that competitive politics could unleash across regions and ethnic groups. Their goal is to be a strong, modern nation governed by a meritocratic Party, not to converge toward Western political norms as outsiders expect.
atomicUnits.sourceBook: One man's view of the world

atomicUnits.title: Demography As National Destiny
atomicUnits.body: A nation's long-term trajectory is constrained by its fertility and population structure: below-replacement birth rates shrink the workforce and burden the young with supporting the old, weakening economic dynamism. Immigration can offset this only if a society is culturally willing to absorb newcomers, which many ageing societies are not.
atomicUnits.sourceBook: One man's view of the world

atomicUnits.title: Judge Nations By Capacity Not Intentions
atomicUnits.body: Strategic assessment should rest on a country's actual capabilities — its economy, military, talent pool, and institutional resilience — rather than on its current rhetoric or stated goodwill. Intentions change overnight while capabilities take decades to build, so durable analysis tracks what a state can do, not what it currently says it wants.
atomicUnits.sourceBook: One man's view of the world

atomicUnits.title: America's Renewal Engine Is Openness
atomicUnits.body: The United States retains primacy because it attracts and absorbs the world's best talent and tolerates restless entrepreneurial failure, continuously regenerating itself. Its capacity for self-criticism and reinvention is a structural advantage that more hierarchical or homogeneous societies cannot easily replicate.
atomicUnits.sourceBook: One man's view of the world

atomicUnits.title: Small State Survival Through Relevance
atomicUnits.body: A small nation has no inherent right to exist and must continuously make itself useful and exceptional — through superior governance, a skilled workforce, and a stable rule of law — to stay valuable to larger powers. Survival depends on being so well-run that bigger players gain more by dealing with you than by absorbing or ignoring you.
atomicUnits.sourceBook: One man's view of the world

atomicUnits.title: Culture Constrains Economic Outcomes
atomicUnits.body: Whether a society prizes saving, education, deferred gratification, and collective discipline shapes its economic performance more than the policies layered on top. Cultures that emphasize these traits accumulate human capital and stability, while those that don't struggle to convert resources or aid into lasting development.
atomicUnits.sourceBook: One man's view of the world

entries.title: Invitation to Courtyard Gardens of Kyoto / 京都坪庭拝見
entries.author: Mizuno Katsuhiko

classification.topic: Photographic Survey of Kyoto's Tsuboniwa Courtyard Gardens
classification.subject: japanese-garden-design

atomicUnits.title: The Tsuboniwa as Light and Air Well
atomicUnits.body: A tsuboniwa is a tiny courtyard garden enclosed by the walls of a single building, originally created to bring light and ventilation into the deep, narrow machiya townhouses of Kyoto. Its primary function is environmental — it acts as a breathing aperture in an otherwise sealed structure — and its aesthetic role grows from solving that practical constraint.
atomicUnits.sourceBook: Invitation to Courtyard Gardens of Kyoto / 京都坪庭拝見

atomicUnits.title: Smallness Intensifies Rather Than Limits Garden Experience
atomicUnits.body: Because a courtyard garden is viewed at close range from fixed interior vantage points, every element — a single stone, a basin, a patch of moss — carries disproportionate weight and must withstand sustained scrutiny. The constraint of extreme smallness forces a concentration of meaning that a large landscape garden can dilute across distance.
atomicUnits.sourceBook: Invitation to Courtyard Gardens of Kyoto / 京都坪庭拝見

atomicUnits.title: Garden Framed by Interior Architecture
atomicUnits.body: A courtyard garden is never seen whole or from outside; it is always composed through the rectangle of a doorway, window, or veranda, so the building's openings become the frame that dictates the picture. Reading the garden means reading the architectural aperture and seating position that determine which fragment of it the viewer ever receives.
atomicUnits.sourceBook: Invitation to Courtyard Gardens of Kyoto / 京都坪庭拝見

atomicUnits.title: Seasonal Marker in Enclosed Space
atomicUnits.body: The courtyard garden imports the passage of seasons into a fully enclosed urban interior through a few deliberately chosen plants whose foliage, blossom, or bare branches signal the time of year. A handful of living elements substitute for an absent open sky, making weather and season perceptible to occupants who rarely step outside.
atomicUnits.sourceBook: Invitation to Courtyard Gardens of Kyoto / 京都坪庭拝見

entries.title: Kyōto, zendera no meitei
entries.author: Katsuhiko Mizuno

classification.topic: Photographic Survey of Famous Zen Temple Gardens in Kyoto
classification.subject: japanese-garden-aesthetics
classification.author_tradition: Japanese photographic documentation of cultural heritage

atomicUnits.title: Borrowed Scenery as Garden Boundary Dissolution
atomicUnits.body: A garden can incorporate distant mountains, trees, or sky outside its walls into its composition, treating the external landscape as an intentional part of the design. This technique, shakkei, makes a bounded space feel limitless by visually annexing what cannot be owned.

atomicUnits.title: Dry Landscape as Abstract Representation
atomicUnits.body: Raked gravel and carefully placed stones can stand in for water, islands, and mountains without any actual water present, forcing the viewer to complete the scene through imagination. The garden becomes a meditative object precisely because it withholds literal depiction and demands interpretive participation.

atomicUnits.title: Seasonal Change Is Designed Into the Garden
atomicUnits.body: Zen gardens are composed to present deliberately different faces across seasons — moss in rain, foliage in autumn, snow on stone — so that no single photograph captures the complete work. The garden is therefore a temporal artwork that exists fully only across the cycle of a year.

atomicUnits.title: Photography as Survey of Designed Stillness
atomicUnits.body: Systematically documenting many temple gardens through fixed images reveals shared structural principles — viewing axes, framed sightlines, asymmetric stone placement — that recur across independent sites. Treating individual gardens as comparable specimens exposes a grammar of design invisible when each is experienced alone.

entries.reviewReason: Only the partial cover edge is visible on the right, text is cropped and faint.; possible sibling of existing entry — verify edition; no Wikipedia entry found — decomposition based on description only

entries.title: 京都・禅寺の名庭 / Samadhi on Zen Gardens: Dynamism and Tranquility
entries.author: MIZUNO Katsuhiko, Tom WRIGHT

classification.topic: Photographic Survey of Kyoto Zen Temple Gardens & Their Aesthetics
classification.subject: eastern-philosophy-zen
classification.author_tradition: Japanese garden photography and Zen aesthetics

atomicUnits.title: Borrowed Scenery as Spatial Extension
atomicUnits.body: Shakkei (borrowed scenery) incorporates distant mountains or trees outside the garden into its composition by framing them through deliberate sightlines and foreground hedges. The garden's apparent size and depth expand far beyond its physical boundaries because the eye reads the borrowed background as part of the designed whole.
atomicUnits.sourceBook: 京都・禅寺の名庭 / Samadhi on Zen Gardens: Dynamism and Tranquility

atomicUnits.title: Raked Gravel as Abstracted Water
atomicUnits.body: In karesansui (dry landscape) gardens, raked white gravel represents flowing water, oceans, or rivers without any actual water present. The mind completes the metaphor, so a still surface of stone evokes motion and vastness, allowing dynamism and tranquility to coexist in the same static field.
atomicUnits.sourceBook: 京都・禅寺の名庭 / Samadhi on Zen Gardens: Dynamism and Tranquility

atomicUnits.title: Emptiness Directs Contemplative Attention
atomicUnits.body: Large areas of deliberate emptiness — bare gravel, blank wall, unplanted space — are not omissions but active design elements that focus the viewer's attention on the few placed objects. The void carries as much intentional meaning as the rocks, forcing the mind toward stillness rather than distraction.
atomicUnits.sourceBook: 京都・禅寺の名庭 / Samadhi on Zen Gardens: Dynamism and Tranquility

atomicUnits.title: Garden as Meditation Object Not Scene
atomicUnits.body: A Zen garden is designed to be viewed from a fixed seated position rather than walked through, turning it into an aid for seated meditation rather than a place for recreation. Reading any designed environment by asking 'what mental state does this posture and vantage induce?' reveals intent invisible to the casual observer.
atomicUnits.sourceBook: 京都・禅寺の名庭 / Samadhi on Zen Gardens: Dynamism and Tranquility

atomicUnits.title: Asymmetry and Odd-Number Grouping
atomicUnits.body: Stones and plantings are arranged in odd-numbered clusters (three, five, seven) and deliberately avoid symmetry or central alignment. Asymmetric balance feels alive and unresolved, preventing the static dead-feeling of mirror symmetry while still achieving compositional unity.
atomicUnits.sourceBook: 京都・禅寺の名庭 / Samadhi on Zen Gardens: Dynamism and Tranquility

atomicUnits.title: Seasonal Impermanence Built Into Design
atomicUnits.body: Gardens are composed so that moss, maples, and moss-covered stone reveal change across seasons and decades, making transience itself part of the aesthetic experience. The design assumes its own aging and weathering as features rather than decay to be prevented.
atomicUnits.sourceBook: 京都・禅寺の名庭 / Samadhi on Zen Gardens: Dynamism and Tranquility

entries.title: 日本の酒 / Sake: A beyond-the-basics guide to Understanding, Tasting, Selection & Enjoyment
entries.author: John Gauntner, Ushihara Mayumi

classification.topic: Japanese Sake Appreciation, Tasting & Selection Guide
classification.subject: sake-tasting-appreciation
classification.author_tradition: American sake expertise & Japanese brewing tradition

atomicUnits.title: Seimaibuai: Rice Polishing Determines Grade
atomicUnits.body: The percentage of the rice grain remaining after polishing (seimaibuai) directly drives sake classification, because the outer layers contain proteins and fats that create off-flavors while the starchy core yields cleaner, more refined sake. A 50% polishing ratio (daiginjo) means half the grain was ground away to reach the pure center, demanding more rice, time, and cost than a 70% ratio.
atomicUnits.sourceBook: 日本の酒 / Sake: A beyond-the-basics guide to Understanding, Tasting, Selection & Enjoyment

atomicUnits.title: Parallel Fermentation as Sake's Distinguishing Mechanism
atomicUnits.body: Sake is produced by multiple parallel fermentation, where koji mold converts rice starch into sugar at the same time yeast converts that sugar into alcohol within a single tank. This simultaneous saccharification-and-fermentation lets sake reach alcohol levels (around 20%) higher than any other naturally brewed beverage, distinguishing it structurally from beer, where these steps occur sequentially.
atomicUnits.sourceBook: 日本の酒 / Sake: A beyond-the-basics guide to Understanding, Tasting, Selection & Enjoyment

atomicUnits.title: Added Alcohol Improves Rather Than Cheapens Sake
atomicUnits.body: Adding a small amount of distilled alcohol during brewing (honjozo-style) is not a cost-cutting adulteration but a deliberate technique that draws out aromatics and produces a lighter, crisper finish. The pejorative association with added alcohol comes from wartime-era practices of bulking volume, which differs entirely from the precise modern brewer's-alcohol additions used for flavor refinement.
atomicUnits.sourceBook: 日本の酒 / Sake: A beyond-the-basics guide to Understanding, Tasting, Selection & Enjoyment

entries.title: RIMOWA - Magazine B Issue No.32
entries.author: JOH & Company (Magazine B)

classification.topic: Brand Profile of RIMOWA's Heritage Luggage Craftsmanship & Identity
classification.subject: brand-design-strategy

atomicUnits.title: The Groove as Permanent Signature
atomicUnits.body: RIMOWA's parallel grooves originated as a structural solution to reinforce thin aluminum sheets against bending, but became the brand's most recognizable visual identity. A functional engineering decision, once distinctive, hardens into an ownable design language that competitors cannot replicate without imitating.

atomicUnits.title: Material Choice Defines Brand Trajectory
atomicUnits.body: RIMOWA's pivot to aluminum after a 1937 fire destroyed its wooden inventory forced a reinvention that aligned the brand with lightweight durability and aviation-era modernity. The constraint imposed by material loss became the organizing principle for everything the company would later stand for.

atomicUnits.title: Luggage as Travel Companion, Not Container
atomicUnits.body: RIMOWA frames its products through the dents and scratches accumulated over years of travel, treating wear as a record of experience rather than damage. Viewing an object as an accumulating biography rather than a depreciating asset reframes durability as emotional value, not just utility.

atomicUnits.title: Heritage Preserved Through Manufacturing Discipline
atomicUnits.body: RIMOWA maintains craftsmanship credibility by keeping aluminum case production labor-intensive and partly manual even at scale, with each case passing through dozens of hand-checked steps. Protecting a slower, more difficult process becomes the proof of authenticity that justifies premium positioning against mass-produced alternatives.

entries.title: Magazine B - Issue No.53 MUJI
entries.author: Magazine B (Brand Balance)

classification.topic: Brand Documentary of MUJI's Minimalist Design Philosophy & Retail Identity
classification.subject: branding-brand-identity

atomicUnits.title: Rationalized Pricing Through Eliminated Excess
atomicUnits.body: MUJI's 'no-brand' positioning works by stripping away decorative packaging, brand markings, and superfluous processing steps, then passing those savings to the customer. The value proposition is not cheapness but justified pricing — every cost that remains corresponds to a function the user actually receives.

atomicUnits.title: This Will Do Over This Is Best
atomicUnits.body: MUJI deliberately aims for rational satisfaction ('this is enough') rather than desire-driven preference ('this is the best'), positioning restraint as the emotional core of the brand. By targeting contentment instead of aspiration, the product avoids the escalating dissatisfaction that comes from chasing maximalism.

atomicUnits.title: Emptiness As A Vessel For Meaning
atomicUnits.body: Designing a product or identity to be intentionally blank lets the user project their own context and use onto it, increasing rather than limiting its versatility. Absence of strong design becomes an active design choice — the void invites participation instead of dictating it.

atomicUnits.title: Process Visibility Becomes The Aesthetic
atomicUnits.body: Rather than hiding manufacturing realities, MUJI exposes them — uneven paper edges, irregular natural materials, and unbleached fibers are kept rather than corrected. The argument is that revealing the honest origin of a thing is more compelling than masking it with artificial uniformity.

entries.reviewReason: enrichment lookup returned no results; no Wikipedia entry found — decomposition based on description only

entries.title: Magazine B - MAISON
entries.author: Magazine B (Brand Balance)

classification.topic: Brand Documentary of MAISON Home & Lifestyle Brand
classification.subject: brand-design-culture

atomicUnits.title: Editorial Consistency Builds Brand Authority
atomicUnits.body: A brand earns trust not by maximizing product variety but by maintaining a tightly curated, coherent point of view across every object it sells. When each item visibly belongs to the same aesthetic family, the curation itself becomes the product, signaling judgment customers can outsource their own taste to.

atomicUnits.title: The Home as Total Lifestyle Canvas
atomicUnits.body: Treating the household as a single integrated environment lets a brand expand from one category into furniture, tableware, textiles, and scent without losing identity. The unifying thread is not the product type but the daily ritual it serves, so coherence comes from the life being lived rather than the shelf the item sits on.

atomicUnits.title: Physical Space as Brand Storytelling Medium
atomicUnits.body: A flagship store or showroom functions less as a sales floor and more as a three-dimensional editorial spread, where objects are staged in lived contexts rather than displayed by category. Customers learn the brand's worldview by walking through assembled scenes, absorbing how products combine before they ever consider buying one.

atomicUnits.title: Timelessness Over Trend Velocity
atomicUnits.body: Evaluating products by how long they will remain relevant, rather than how well they capture the current moment, shifts design toward durability of both materials and aesthetics. This reframes pricing and slowness as features: the customer pays more upfront for objects intended to outlast seasonal cycles and accumulate meaning over time.

entries.title: BRAND. BALANCE. ISSUE NO.56 MICHELIN GUIDE
entries.author: Magazine B

classification.topic: Michelin Brand Identity & Hospitality Reputation Building
classification.subject: branding-brand-strategy

atomicUnits.title: Authority Borrowed From an Unrelated Core Business
atomicUnits.body: A tire manufacturer created a restaurant rating system to encourage driving, which would wear out tires and sell more product. The credibility came precisely because the rater had no stake in the food industry, making its judgments appear disinterested and therefore trustworthy.
atomicUnits.sourceBook: BRAND. BALANCE. ISSUE NO.56 MICHELIN GUIDE

atomicUnits.title: Anonymous Repeat Evaluation as Trust Engine
atomicUnits.body: Inspectors visit and pay as ordinary customers, return multiple times, and never reveal themselves, so establishments cannot perform for the judge. Reputation rests on the consistency of the everyday experience rather than a curated showcase moment.
atomicUnits.sourceBook: BRAND. BALANCE. ISSUE NO.56 MICHELIN GUIDE

atomicUnits.title: Scarcity of Stars Protects the Currency
atomicUnits.body: By withholding the top rating from the vast majority and removing stars when standards slip, the system keeps each award rare and meaningful. The willingness to revoke recognition is what makes granting it valuable.
atomicUnits.sourceBook: BRAND. BALANCE. ISSUE NO.56 MICHELIN GUIDE

atomicUnits.title: Rating Tiers as Differentiated Promises
atomicUnits.body: Separating distinctions like the star tiers from the budget-friendly recommendation lets a single brand certify quality across price points without diluting its premium signal. Each tier communicates a distinct kind of value rather than a single hierarchy of better-or-worse.
atomicUnits.sourceBook: BRAND. BALANCE. ISSUE NO.56 MICHELIN GUIDE

entries.title: France
entries.author: Michelin

classification.topic: Travel Guide to France
classification.subject: france-travel-guide

atomicUnits.title: Star rating to ration limited attention
atomicUnits.body: Rank attractions on a three-tier scale — worth a journey, worth a detour, interesting — so a traveler with finite time can allocate it by expected payoff rather than reacting to whatever is nearby. The rating compresses expert judgment into a single glyph that filters thousands of options down to a feasible itinerary.

atomicUnits.title: Place understood through accumulated layers
atomicUnits.body: A location is read not as a current snapshot but as sediment — Roman walls under medieval streets under modern facades — where each era left physical traces that explain why the present looks as it does. Seeing a site this way turns a static view into a sequence of decisions and disruptions still legible in the stone.

atomicUnits.title: Terroir as region-bound character
atomicUnits.body: Food, wine, and craft acquire qualities that cannot be reproduced elsewhere because they emerge from a specific combination of soil, climate, and local technique. This binds a product's identity to its place of origin, making the region itself a guarantee of distinctiveness.

entries.reviewReason: Only the top edges of red and green Michelin guides are visible at the top of the image.

entries.title: NETFLIX
entries.author: Magazine B

classification.topic: Brand Study of Netflix as a Streaming and Content Company
classification.subject: brand-business-strategy

atomicUnits.title: Freedom And Responsibility Over Process
atomicUnits.body: Instead of controlling employees with rules and approval chains, give high-performing people maximum autonomy and hold them accountable for outcomes. As a company scales, the instinct is to add process to prevent errors, but this drives out top talent; the alternative is to increase talent density so freedom can scale alongside the company.

atomicUnits.title: Recommendation Algorithm As Core Product
atomicUnits.body: The value of a streaming service is not the size of its library but its ability to surface the right title to each individual viewer at the right moment. Personalized recommendation reduces the friction of choice and becomes the primary engine of retention, making the algorithm itself a central product rather than a supporting feature.

atomicUnits.title: Vertical Integration Into Original Content
atomicUnits.body: A distribution platform reduces its dependence on licensors by producing its own content, capturing both the audience relationship and the intellectual property. Original production turns a reseller of others' work into an owner of exclusive assets that cannot be pulled away by competitors or rising licensing costs.

atomicUnits.title: Releasing Entire Seasons Reshapes Consumption
atomicUnits.body: Dropping a full season at once rather than weekly episodes changes how stories are consumed, enabling binge-watching as a behavior and a cultural event. This shifts the unit of attention from the single episode to the whole series and aligns release strategy with viewer control rather than broadcaster scheduling.

entries.title: B Magazine Issue No.54 Maison Margiela
entries.author: Brand Documentary Magazine (BRAND. BALANCE.)

classification.topic: Profile of Maison Margiela as a Fashion Brand & Design Philosophy
classification.subject: fashion-brand-design

atomicUnits.title: Anonymity As Brand Signature
atomicUnits.body: By refusing to appear in photographs, conducting interviews only by fax, and hiding designers behind a collective 'we,' the brand makes absence itself the recognizable mark. The deliberate erasure of the individual creator paradoxically becomes the most identifiable feature, shifting attention from personality to the work itself.
atomicUnits.sourceBook: B Magazine Issue No.54 Maison Margiela

atomicUnits.title: The Blank White Label
atomicUnits.body: An unmarked white cotton rectangle attached with four exposed corner stitches functions as a logo precisely by carrying no name. The visible external stitching turns an internal construction element into a public identifier, so the garment announces its origin through method rather than text.
atomicUnits.sourceBook: B Magazine Issue No.54 Maison Margiela

atomicUnits.title: Deconstruction As Design Method
atomicUnits.body: Garments are built by exposing what is normally concealed — showing seams, leaving hems raw, displaying linings and shoulder pads on the outside. Revealing the process of making becomes the aesthetic, treating the unfinished and the structural as the finished surface.
atomicUnits.sourceBook: B Magazine Issue No.54 Maison Margiela

atomicUnits.title: Recycling Discards Into Couture
atomicUnits.body: Existing objects — vintage clothing, broken plates, military socks, theater costumes — are reassembled into new garments rather than fabricated from raw cloth. This reframes value as residing in transformation and recontextualization, so that what was thrown away becomes the raw material of luxury.
atomicUnits.sourceBook: B Magazine Issue No.54 Maison Margiela

entries.title: MCDP 1-1 STRATEGY
entries.author: Department of Defense

classification.topic: Military Doctrine on the Theory & Practice of Strategy
classification.subject: military-strategy-doctrine

atomicUnits.title: Strategy as a Two-Player Interactive Game
atomicUnits.body: Strategy is fundamentally different from other problem-solving because it operates against a hostile, independent will that adapts and reacts to your moves. Success therefore depends not on executing a fixed plan but on out-thinking and out-adapting an opponent who is simultaneously trying to do the same to you.

atomicUnits.title: Ends, Ways, and Means Alignment
atomicUnits.body: Sound strategy requires matching political objectives (ends) with chosen methods (ways) and available resources (means), and the three must remain in balance. When objectives outstrip resources or methods cannot achieve the goals, strategy fails regardless of tactical excellence on the battlefield.

atomicUnits.title: War as Continuation of Policy
atomicUnits.body: Military force is not an end in itself but an instrument subordinate to political purpose, meaning every act of violence must serve a defined political aim. Viewing conflict this way reveals that purely military victory is meaningless if it does not advance the political conditions you actually want.

entries.title: Tao te Ching
entries.author: 老子

classification.topic: Taoist Philosophy & Cosmological Ethics
classification.subject: eastern-philosophy-taoism

atomicUnits.title: Wu Wei: Action Through Non-Forcing
atomicUnits.body: The most effective action is non-coercive action that aligns with the natural flow of a situation rather than imposing will against it. By not striving, interfering, or grasping, one accomplishes more than through aggressive effort — the way water carves stone by yielding.

atomicUnits.title: The Nameable Tao Is Not The Real One
atomicUnits.body: Any reality that can be fully named, defined, or captured in concepts is not the ultimate reality itself, only a pointer to it. Language and categories carve the world into pieces, but the underlying source is continuous and indivisible, so precise definition always distorts what it describes.

atomicUnits.title: Power Of The Empty And Useful Void
atomicUnits.body: Utility comes from emptiness, not substance: a wheel's hub, a vessel's hollow, and a room's empty space are what make them functional. What is absent or unfilled is as essential as what is present, so value often resides in negative space rather than material.

atomicUnits.title: Leading By Becoming Invisible
atomicUnits.body: The best leader is one whose presence is barely felt, so that when goals are met people say 'we did this ourselves.' Authority is strengthened by stepping back, sharing credit, and refraining from micromanagement, while controlling, self-promoting leadership breeds resistance.

atomicUnits.title: Strength Through Softness And Lowliness
atomicUnits.body: Soft, yielding, low-positioned things outlast and overcome hard, rigid, high-positioned ones — water defeats rock, the supple survive while the brittle break. Viewing situations this way reframes apparent weakness as durable advantage and apparent dominance as fragility.

atomicUnits.title: Opposites Define And Generate Each Other
atomicUnits.body: Concepts like beauty and ugliness, difficulty and ease, high and low exist only in mutual dependence; recognizing one creates the other. Reality is structured by complementary pairs rather than absolute categories, so pursuing one extreme inevitably summons its opposite.

entries.reviewReason: Author not printed on cover; inferred from the canonical work.; possible sibling of existing entry — verify edition

entries.title: Lun yu
entries.author: Confucius

classification.topic: Confucian Ethics & Political Philosophy
classification.subject: eastern-philosophy-taoism

atomicUnits.title: Rectification of Names
atomicUnits.body: Social disorder begins when words no longer match reality — when a ruler is not ruling, a father not fathering. Restoring order requires first making each role-name correspond to its actual conduct, because language that drifts from truth makes coordinated action and just punishment impossible.

atomicUnits.title: Reciprocity as the Negative Golden Rule
atomicUnits.body: The single thread that runs through ethical life is shu: do not impose on others what you would not want imposed on yourself. This restraint-based formulation governs behavior by checking action against one's own aversions rather than prescribing positive deeds.

atomicUnits.title: Rule by Virtue Over Punishment
atomicUnits.body: If you govern people by laws and keep order by punishments, they avoid wrongdoing but feel no shame. If you govern by moral force and order by ritual propriety, they develop a sense of shame and correct themselves from within.

atomicUnits.title: Ritual Propriety as Behavioral Scaffolding
atomicUnits.body: Li — codified ritual and etiquette — channels raw feeling into appropriate form, training character through repeated correct practice rather than abstract belief. Even virtues like courage or honesty become harmful without ritual to shape and restrain them.

atomicUnits.title: Government as Extension of Family
atomicUnits.body: Political loyalty and filial piety are not separate domains but a single continuum: one who honors parents and respects elders rarely rebels against authority. The cultivated household becomes the training ground and template for ordering the state.

atomicUnits.title: The Junzi as Self-Cultivating Exemplar
atomicUnits.body: The noble person is defined not by birth but by continuous moral self-improvement, judging himself while making no demands on others. He is at ease without arrogance, seeks rightness rather than profit, and influences others by example as wind bends grass.

entries.reviewReason: Author not printed on cover; inferred from the work.; no Wikipedia entry found — decomposition based on description only

entries.title: The Great Learning
entries.author: Confucius/Zengzi (attributed)

classification.topic: Confucian Self-Cultivation & Moral Governance
classification.subject: confucian-philosophy
subtopics: Investigation of Things & Rectification of Mind

atomicUnits.title: The Eight Connected Steps of Cultivation
atomicUnits.body: Order in the world is built backward from the self: investigate things, extend knowledge, make intentions sincere, rectify the heart, cultivate the person, regulate the family, govern the state, then bring peace to the world. Each link depends on the one before it, so large-scale order is impossible without first ordering the smallest interior unit.

atomicUnits.title: Personal Cultivation Is the Universal Root
atomicUnits.body: From the ruler down to the common person, self-cultivation is the single foundation on which everything else rests. When the root is in disorder, no well-ordered branches can grow — a corrupted self cannot produce a well-governed household or state.

atomicUnits.title: Investigation of Things Precedes Knowledge
atomicUnits.body: Genuine knowledge is not given but extended by examining the actual things and affairs of the world. Only after one exhaustively investigates particulars does understanding become complete enough to act rightly.

atomicUnits.title: Sincerity as Not Self-Deceiving
atomicUnits.body: Making intentions sincere means refusing to deceive oneself, like hating a bad smell or loving a beautiful sight — the response must be automatic and unfeigned. The cultivated person is therefore watchful when alone, because integrity is measured by what one does when unobserved.

atomicUnits.title: The Heart's Distortion of Perception
atomicUnits.body: When the mind is gripped by anger, fear, fondness, or anxiety, it loses its correctness and the person fails to perceive accurately — looking without seeing, eating without tasting. Rectifying the heart means clearing these emotional disturbances so that perception and judgment remain undistorted.

atomicUnits.title: Virtue Attracts People, People Attract Wealth
atomicUnits.body: A ruler should treat virtue as the root and wealth as the branch; accumulating riches while neglecting character scatters the people, while accumulating virtue gathers them. Possessing the people yields territory, territory yields wealth, and wealth yields use — so moral character is the true engine of prosperity.

entries.reviewReason: Authorship traditionally attributed and disputed; not printed on cover.; enrichment lookup returned no results

entries.title: The Doctrine of the Mean
entries.author: Zisi (attributed)

classification.topic: Confucian Ethics of Balance, Harmony & the Cultivation of the Superior Person
classification.subject: confucian-ethics-self-cultivation
subtopics: Harmony Between Heaven and Human Conduct

atomicUnits.title: The Mean as Constant Equilibrium
atomicUnits.body: The Mean is not a fixed midpoint but the state of holding to what is right and unchanging under shifting circumstances — adjusting action to fit the situation without swerving into excess or deficiency. It is difficult precisely because it requires continual recalibration rather than a single rule applied mechanically.

atomicUnits.title: Harmony Follows From Regulated Emotion
atomicUnits.body: When feelings like joy and anger have not yet arisen, the mind is in equilibrium; when they arise and all hit their proper measure, that is harmony. Achieving this internal state is what makes order in the external world possible, so self-regulation precedes social peace.

atomicUnits.title: Sincerity as the Engine of Development
atomicUnits.body: Sincerity (cheng) is the full realization of one's nature, and through it a person can fully develop the natures of others and of all things. The sequence runs from being sincere with oneself, to influencing others, to participating in the transforming and nourishing operations of heaven and earth.

atomicUnits.title: Cultivation Begins Where Unseen
atomicUnits.body: The superior person is watchful over themselves when alone, because moral character forms in the hidden and the minute where nothing is more visible than what is concealed. Integrity is measured by conduct when no one observes, not by public performance.

atomicUnits.title: The Path That Cannot Be Departed From
atomicUnits.body: What heaven confers is human nature, and following that nature is the path — a path so intrinsic that if it could be abandoned it would not be the true path at all. This reframes morality not as external imposition but as the act of staying aligned with what one already is.

atomicUnits.title: Greatness Grows From Near and Low
atomicUnits.body: The way of the superior person works like traveling to a distant place by starting from what is near, or ascending a height by beginning from below. Far-reaching transformation is built incrementally from ordinary, immediate relationships and small consistent acts rather than grand gestures.

entries.reviewReason: Authorship traditionally attributed; not printed on cover.; enrichment lookup returned no results; no Wikipedia entry found — decomposition based on description only

entries.title: The works of Mencius
entries.author: Mencius

classification.topic: Confucian Ethics & Moral Political Philosophy
classification.subject: confucian-philosophy

atomicUnits.title: Human Nature Tends Toward Good
atomicUnits.body: Human nature inclines toward goodness the way water flows downward — given the right conditions, compassion, shame, courtesy, and moral judgment emerge naturally. When people act badly, it is the corrupting force of circumstance, not their original nature, just as water can be splashed uphill only by external force.

atomicUnits.title: The Four Sprouts of Virtue
atomicUnits.body: Every person possesses four 'beginnings' or seeds: the feeling of compassion (sprout of benevolence), the feeling of shame (righteousness), the feeling of deference (propriety), and the sense of right and wrong (wisdom). These exist as undeveloped potentials that must be cultivated and expanded, like a fire starting to burn or a spring starting to flow.

atomicUnits.title: Legitimacy Rests on the People's Welfare
atomicUnits.body: A ruler retains the Mandate of Heaven only by securing the people's material wellbeing and winning their hearts; when a ruler becomes a tyrant who robs and oppresses, he forfeits his title and may justly be removed. Killing such a deposed tyrant is not regicide because he has already ceased to be a true sovereign and become a mere 'fellow.'

atomicUnits.title: The Child About to Fall in a Well
atomicUnits.body: Anyone who suddenly sees a child about to tumble into a well feels an immediate stab of alarm and compassion — not to gain favor with the parents, win a reputation, or because they dislike the child's cry. This spontaneous reaction proves that the impulse to compassion is innate to all humans rather than learned or self-interested.

entries.reviewReason: Author not printed on cover but clearly inferred from the title.; no Wikipedia entry found — decomposition based on description only

entries.title: The Gulistan of Sa'di
entries.author: Translated by W.M. Thackston

classification.topic: Classical Persian moral tales and ethical wisdom in prose and verse
classification.subject: persian-ethical-literature

atomicUnits.title: A Calculated Lie Beats Truthful Strife
atomicUnits.body: When telling the truth produces division, harm, or needless cruelty, a falsehood that restores peace is morally superior to an honesty that inflames conflict. The value of speech is measured by its consequences for harmony, not by literal accuracy alone.

atomicUnits.title: The Condemned Man Who Cursed the King
atomicUnits.body: A prisoner facing execution insults the king, who asks what he said; a kind minister falsely reports the man praised those who restrain anger and forgive others, prompting a pardon, while a rival minister insists on the truth to ensure death. The king favors the merciful lie over the destructive truth, showing that the intent behind speech determines its worth.

atomicUnits.title: Contentment as Freedom From Dependence
atomicUnits.body: Wealth is defined not by possessions but by the absence of wants, so the person who needs little is richer than the person who hoards much. Reducing desire severs the dependence that makes one vulnerable to flattery, servitude, and anxiety.

atomicUnits.title: Character Revealed Through Reversal of Fortune
atomicUnits.body: A person's true nature stays hidden until circumstances flip — the beggar elevated to power and the powerful man brought low expose whether virtue was genuine or merely a product of comfort. Judge people by how they behave when their station suddenly changes, not by their conduct in stable conditions.
